hints: Hours off = 24n × (number of days with at least one birthday).
E[off days]=365(1−(364/365)ⁿ) → 365, so E[hours off]≈24n·365 grows with n.
Unbounded in n ⇒ infinite ⇒ −1.
solution: On a day with at least one birthday all $n$ employees get $24$ hours off, so expected hours off $=24n\cdot365\big(1-(\tfrac{364}{365})^n\big)$. As $n\to\infty$ the bracket $\to1$ and the expression grows like $24\cdot365\,n\to\infty$. There is no maximizer, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: Now it's zero-sum: X's payoff is [x(100−x) − y(100−y)]/(x+y).
At the symmetric equilibrium x=y, ∂/∂x of the numerator gives (100−2x)(2x)=0.
solution: X's expected payoff is $U(x,y)=\dfrac{x(100-x)-y(100-y)}{x+y}$ (X gains when its token is drawn, pays when Y's is). At the symmetric equilibrium $x=y$, $\partial_x U=0$ reduces to $(100-2x)(2x)=0$, so $x=50.$

hints: In the best case the sick count doubles each minute.
Need 2ᵗ ≥ 1000.
2¹⁰=1024 ⇒ 10 minutes.
solution: At best every sick person infects a new healthy one each minute, so the count doubles: $1,2,4,\dots,2^t$. We need $2^t\ge1000$, giving $t=10$ (since $2^{10}=1024$).

hints: Each card is uniform on {0,1,2,3,4}: variance (5²−1)/12=2.
Independent sum ⇒ variance adds: 2+2=4.
solution: Each card is uniform on $\{0,\dots,4\}$ with variance $\tfrac{5^2-1}{12}=2$. The independent sum has variance $2+2=4$, so the standard deviation is $2.$

hints: The allowed moves are cyclic rotations, so only the cyclic order matters.
Of the 6 orderings, the 3 in RGB's cyclic class can be rotated to RGB.
solution: Moving the back car to the front is a cyclic rotation, which preserves cyclic order. Of the $6$ arrangements, exactly the $3$ in the same cyclic class as $RGB$ can be rotated into rainbow order, so $P=\tfrac36=\tfrac12.$

hints: The earth card is irrelevant; consider only air, water, fire.
You win iff fire is last among these three.
P(fire last of 3)=1/3.
A car leads its own cluster iff it's the slowest among all cars ahead of it (a 'record' from the front).
P(car ranked i-th from front leads)=1/i, so E[K]=Σ_{i=1}^{N} 1/i = H_N.
solution: A car heads its own cluster exactly when no car ahead of it is slower — i.e. it is a left-to-right minimum of speeds. The $i$-th car from the front is such a record with probability $\tfrac1i$, so $\mathbb{E}[K]=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\tfrac1i=H_N$. For $N=10$, $H_{10}=\tfrac{7381}{2520}.$

hints: Divisible by 10^80 needs both exponents in {80,…,99}: 20·20=400.
solution: $10^{99}=2^{99}5^{99}$ has $100\cdot100=10000$ divisors. Being divisible by $10^{80}=2^{80}5^{80}$ requires each exponent in $\{80,\dots,99\}$: $20\cdot20=400$ divisors. So $P=\tfrac{400}{10000}=\tfrac1{25}.$

hints: By Itô, d(Wⁿ)=nW^{n−1}dW + (n(n−1)/2)W^{n−2}dt; martingale needs zero drift.
Drift vanishes only for n=0 and n=1.
solution: By Itô, $d(W_t^n)=nW_t^{n-1}dW_t+\tfrac{n(n-1)}2 W_t^{n-2}dt$, which is driftless (a martingale) only for $n=0$ and $n=1$. The sum of factorials is $0!+1!=2.$

hints: E[X²]=Var(X)+(E[X])².
Vega ∝ S·√T·φ(d₁); for ATM options it grows with the price level.
Higher strike means higher underlying (ATM), hence larger vega.
solution: For ATM options, vega $\approx S\sqrt T\,\varphi(d_1)$ scales with the price level $S=K$. The largest strike $K=30$ therefore has the greatest vega.

hints: Distinct pairwise digit sums means the digits form a Sidon (B₂) set within {0,…,9}.
The largest Sidon subset of {0,…,9} has size 5 (e.g. {0,1,2,6,9}).
So n=5.
solution: All pairwise sums distinct means the digits form a Sidon set. The largest Sidon subset of $\{0,\dots,9\}$ has size $5$ (for instance $\{0,1,2,6,9\}$ has all $\binom52=10$ pairwise sums distinct), so the largest such number has $n=5$ digits.
